Defining and counting sepsis hospitalizations 
While sepsis has been broadly defined as "life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection", different approaches are needed to identify sepsis hospitalizations depending on the setting and patient population (e.g., neonatal, pediatric, maternal, adult) 65 66 67 68 69 70 71 . Sepsis programs must also carefully consider how to best define sepsis for the intended purpose, which may include clinical care, research, surveillance, quality improvement, and audit. 65 72 
For example, diagnostic codes may be readily accessible and reflect individual clinical judgments. However, use of diagnostic codes may also be subject to provider, hospital, and temporal variation in coding practices. 73 74 75 76 Furthermore, sepsis quality improvement initiatives that raise provider awareness can lead to stage migration by including more patients with milder disease, thus lower perceived sepsis mortality. 74 77 78 
Definitions that use objective clinical data, such as the CDC Adult Sepsis Event (CDC ASE) criteria, can be used to benchmark sepsis quality improvement initiatives that require a consistent measure that can be extracted from data available in electronic hea
